title: Capture of Jerusalem by the First Crusade
summary: On 15 July 1099, after a five-week siege in high summer, a Frankish army of the First Crusade stormed the walls of Jerusalem and ended four and a half centuries of Muslim rule over the city. The massacre that followed shocked contemporaries and darkened Christian-Muslim relations for generations. Out of the victory the Crusaders carved four Latin states along the eastern Mediterranean coast, opened a new front of contact and conflict between Latin Europe and the Islamic world, and inaugurated the crusading era.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The First Crusade was called into being at Clermont in central France on 27 November 1095, when Pope Urban II preached in an open field to an assembly of French bishops and lords. He answered an appeal from the Byzantine emperor Alexios I Komnenos, whose empire had lost most of Anatolia to Seljuk Turks after the disaster of Manzikert in 1071, and he added a call to rescue the holy places of Jerusalem, which had passed from the Arab Fatimid caliphate to Turkish rulers a generation earlier. In return he offered a plenary indulgence, remission of the temporal penalty of sin, to all who took the cross and reached the sepulchre of Christ.

The response was immense. A first, unsanctioned wave, the so-called People's Crusade led by the charismatic preacher Peter the Hermit, marched east in the spring of 1096, massacred Rhineland Jewish communities on the way, and was largely destroyed by Turkish forces near Nicaea in October. The main princely armies followed later that year and gathered at Constantinople through the winter of 1096 to 1097 under leaders including Godfrey of Bouillon and his brother Baldwin of Boulogne, Bohemond of Taranto and his nephew Tancred, Raymond IV of Toulouse, and Robert II of Normandy, eldest son of William the Conqueror. Total numbers are debated but perhaps fifty thousand fighting men and camp followers crossed the Bosphorus.

The crusaders' route ran through a fractured Muslim world. The Seljuk sultanate had disintegrated after the death of Malik Shah in 1092 into rival emirates from Damascus to Mosul. In July 1098 the Fatimid vizier al-Afdal took advantage of that fragmentation to recapture Jerusalem from its Turkish garrison. It was a Fatimid, not a Turkish, governor who would defend the city against the Franks the following summer.

The crusaders' progress was punishing and improbable. They captured Nicaea in June 1097, won a hard-fought pitched battle at Dorylaeum in July, and endured an eight-month siege of Antioch through the winter of 1097 to 1098 in which starvation reduced them to eating pack animals and, in some accounts, the dead. Antioch fell to Bohemond in June 1098 and a relieving Turkish army was defeated outside the walls with the aid, so participants believed, of the miraculously discovered Holy Lance. After a year's delay caused by disease and squabbling over Antioch, the surviving army, now perhaps twelve to fifteen thousand fighters, marched south along the Levantine coast in the spring of 1099. It reached the walls of Jerusalem on 7 June.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
Jerusalem in the summer of 1099 was defended by a Fatimid garrison under the governor Iftikhar al-Dawla, well provisioned and reinforced by refugees from the surrounding countryside. Before the crusaders' arrival the governor had expelled most of the city's Eastern Christian population, whom he distrusted, poisoned or blocked the wells outside the walls, and driven the flocks into the hills. The Franks arrived exhausted, thirsty, and reduced by disease.

Water was a constant crisis: the nearest safe supply was the pool of Siloam, itself exposed to Fatimid sortie, and animals died so quickly that carcasses fouled the camp. A first attempt to storm the walls with makeshift ladders on 13 June failed with heavy losses. The Franks then set about the slow work of building siege engines. Genoese and English ships arriving at the port of Jaffa contributed rope, nails, and skilled carpenters; timber was hauled from the wooded slopes near Samaria. Under the direction of the engineer Gaston of Bearn two large wooden towers were assembled, one for the northern wall under Godfrey and Robert of Flanders, and one for the southern under Raymond of Toulouse near Mount Zion.

On 8 July, at the urging of the Provencal visionary Peter Desiderius, the entire army walked barefoot in procession around the walls, carrying relics and reliquaries, in imitation of the biblical circuit of Jericho. The Fatimid defenders reportedly jeered from the ramparts. Six days later, on the night of 13 to 14 July, the crusaders quietly moved Godfrey's siege tower to a section of the northern wall opposite the Quadrangular Tower, which the defenders had left less heavily engined because it had seemed too far to attack.

The assault began on Thursday 14 July. All that day and into the next Fatimid mangonels, arrows, and jars of naphtha, or Greek fire, were poured onto the crusader towers, while the crusaders shot back and battered the walls with their own artillery. Around noon on Friday 15 July, a knight of Godfrey's party, later identified as Ludolf of Tournai, managed to lay a bridge from the top of the tower onto the wall. Frankish troops poured onto the ramparts and threw open the Gate of the Column on the north wall; at the same time the crusaders under Tancred forced the Temple Mount district. Raymond's men entered by the southern citadel, later called the Tower of David, after Iftikhar al-Dawla surrendered it in return for safe conduct to Ascalon.

What followed was among the most notorious atrocities of the medieval period. For at least two days Frankish soldiers massacred Muslim and Jewish inhabitants across the city. Fulcher of Chartres, who arrived shortly afterwards, wrote of piles of hands and feet in the streets; the Provencal chronicler Raymond of Aguilers, in an image often quoted, described knights riding through the Temple precinct in blood up to their horses' knees. Jews who took refuge in the main synagogue were burned inside it. Modern historians treat the more extreme details as rhetorical exaggeration but agree that the killing was vast, indiscriminate, and continued after resistance had ceased.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
On 22 July the surviving princes elected Godfrey of Bouillon as ruler of the new state. He declined the title of king in the city where Christ had worn a crown of thorns and took instead the title Advocate of the Holy Sepulchre. Three weeks later, on 12 August 1099, he defeated a Fatimid relief army under al-Afdal at Ascalon, securing the conquest. Godfrey died the following July and his brother Baldwin succeeded him without the same religious scruple, having himself crowned Baldwin I, King of Jerusalem, on Christmas Day 1100 in the Church of the Nativity at Bethlehem.

Four Latin states emerged from the crusade: the County of Edessa, established by Baldwin in 1098; the Principality of Antioch, held by Bohemond; the County of Tripoli, carved out by Raymond of Toulouse and his heirs; and the Kingdom of Jerusalem itself. Together they constituted Outremer, the lands beyond the sea, a French-speaking crusader society perched precariously on the Levantine coast for the next two centuries.

Muslim reactions were slow but eventually decisive. Contemporaries in Baghdad and Damascus were dismayed but internally divided, and the Fatimid caliphate never regained the initiative. It was the Turkish atabeg Zengi of Mosul who recaptured Edessa in 1144, triggering the failed Second Crusade, and his son Nur al-Din who unified Muslim Syria. Nur al-Din's Kurdish general Saladin took Egypt in 1171, put an end to the Fatimid caliphate, and in 1187 crushed the crusader field army at Hattin and retook Jerusalem itself. Within a century of 1099 the city was again in Muslim hands, but the crusading movement it had inaugurated would echo through European politics for centuries.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The First Crusade set patterns that shaped Latin Christian culture for the rest of the Middle Ages. It gave the papacy a new instrument of leadership and unified western knightly society around a shared, if often violent, religious project. It produced the first military religious orders, the Templars founded around 1119 and the Hospitallers not long after, part monastic community and part standing army, which would become major landholders across Europe.

Economically the crusader states drew Italian maritime republics into permanent Levantine commerce: Venice, Genoa, and Pisa obtained quarters in Acre, Tyre, and Jaffa, and their long-distance trade in spices, sugar, silk, and glass helped fuel the twelfth-century European economic revival. Techniques of stone castle-building, hydraulic engineering, and mounted warfare crossed both ways between Latin and Levantine societies.

The darker legacies were equally lasting. The anti-Jewish massacres of 1096 in the Rhineland and 1099 in Jerusalem inaugurated centuries of episodic pogroms in medieval Europe. The crusade fixed in western Christian memory a heroic story of holy war for Jerusalem that would be invoked by later popes and, in modern times, by nationalist and colonial rhetorics. It is remembered on the other side of the Mediterranean, in Arabic and later Turkish historiography, as an early trauma in a much longer engagement between the Islamic world and the Christian west. The July 1099 storming of Jerusalem remains one of the single most consequential and most contested events of the entire medieval period.

worldContext.text: Northern Song China under Emperor Zhezong was governed by a court divided between the reformist New Policies of Wang Anshi's heirs and their conservative opponents. Kaifeng was the greatest commercial city on earth, its population well past a million, with fire brigades, printed books, and paper money in general use. To the north the Khitan Liao and Tangut Western Xia held the frontier steppes; Jurchen tribes further north were still nominal Liao subjects.

In northern India the Chahamana and Chalukya kingdoms of Rajasthan and Gujarat were at their height under rulers like Vigraharaja IV and Karna. Ghaznavid raids from the northwest had eased since Mahmud's death, but the Ghurids who would eventually replace them were on the rise in the highlands of central Afghanistan. In the south the Chola king Kulottunga I ruled a maritime empire whose ambassadors reached Song China.

The Almoravid Berber empire ruled Morocco, most of al-Andalus, and the western Sahara, and had checked the Christian Reconquista at Zallaqa in 1086. In West Africa the empire of Ghana was in decline as trans-Saharan gold caravans increasingly bypassed it. In the Ethiopian highlands the Zagwe dynasty, soon to build the rock-cut churches of Lalibela, replaced the older Aksumite line.

In central Mexico the Toltec city of Tula was near its zenith, its distinctive warrior columns and colonnaded courts influencing far-off Chichen Itza in the Yucatan. In the American Southwest the Chaco Canyon system in modern New Mexico was in the middle of its great construction period, with Pueblo Bonito and Chetro Ketl rising as multi-storey great houses. Along the Mississippi the city of Cahokia was beginning its rapid growth.

Polynesian voyaging had populated most of the eastern Pacific; permanent Maori settlement of New Zealand was still perhaps a century away. Rapa Nui, Hawaii, and the Society Islands were inhabited farming societies. In Australia Aboriginal communities continued sophisticated land management, and in highland New Guinea intensive taro and yam agriculture had supported dense settlement for millennia.

figures.name: Pope Urban II
figures.role: Reformist pope who preached the crusade at Clermont in November 1095; died on 29 July 1099, before news of Jerusalem's capture reached Rome

figures.name: Godfrey of Bouillon
figures.role: Duke of Lower Lorraine, first Latin ruler of Jerusalem as Advocate of the Holy Sepulchre from 1099 to 1100
figures.credit: Fresco at Castello della Manta, early 15th century, via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Raymond IV of Toulouse
figures.role: Wealthiest crusade leader; commanded the southern siege tower and later founded the County of Tripoli

figures.name: Bohemond of Taranto
figures.role: Norman prince who broke out of the Byzantine oath and made himself Prince of Antioch after its capture in 1098

figures.name: Iftikhar al-Dawla
figures.role: Fatimid governor of Jerusalem; surrendered the citadel to Raymond in exchange for safe conduct to Ascalon

images.caption: The Siege of Jerusalem in 1099, from a 14th-century manuscript of William of Tyre's Historia. Bibliotheque nationale de France, Ms. Francais 2824, folio 45.
images.credit: Anonymous medieval French illuminator, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: Godfrey of Bouillon as one of the Nine Worthies, fresco in the baronial hall of Castello della Manta in Piedmont, early 15th century.
images.credit: Anonymous early 15th-century fresco painter, via Wikimedia Commons
